subtopics.subtopic_title: TUBERCULOSIS SCREENING AND NTEP LINKAGE IN NONRESPONDERS
subtopics.concept: In cases of severe acute malnutrition (SAM), a lack of response to therapeutic interventions in paediatric wards should prompt a structured evaluation for tuberculosis (TB) rather than repeated escalation of nutritional feeds alone. TB should be suspected in non-responding children who have a history of contact with TB, chronic cough, persistent fever, lymphadenopathy, weight loss, or failure to gain weight despite verified therapeutic feeding and control of acute infections. It is essential to interpret diagnostic tests while considering the physiological effects of malnutrition, as severe malnutrition can lead to false-negative tuberculin skin tests (TST) due to secondary immunodeficiency. Therefore, a negative TST result should not rule out TB in high-risk, non-responding children. A syndrome-based approach is recommended, which includes reviewing chest symptoms and examination, obtaining a chest radiograph when available, evaluating for extrapulmonary TB, and considering concurrent HIV testing in alignment with national algorithms. When TB is suspected, it is critical to collect microbiological specimens, such as gastric aspirates, lavage, or induced sputum, following paediatric protocols. These specimens should be sent to a linked diagnostic facility through the National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP) network. Coordination with the facility’s Nutrition Rehabilitation Centre (NRC) team is essential to ensure the child is logged as a presumptive TB case, and test results are actively tracked. Non-responding children must not be discharged without a documented TB management plan. Screening and referral actions should be meticulously recorded in the ward records to avoid losing critical information during patient transfer. Caregivers must be informed about when and where to return for test results. If TB treatment is initiated, pyridoxine (vitamin B6) prophylaxis at a dose of 10 mg/day should be provided alongside isoniazid-containing regimens to mitigate the increased risk of neuropathy associated with malnutrition. Slower weight recovery should be anticipated, necessitating close follow-up between nutrition services and the NTEP after discharge to ensure comprehensive care.

case_studies.title: Non-Responder in Severe Acute Malnutrition: Evaluating for Tuberculosis
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India is admitted to the paediatric ward with severe acute malnutrition (weight-for-height <-3 Z-scores) and bilateral pedal edema. He has been receiving therapeutic feeds for 10 days but shows no significant weight gain or clinical improvement. His mother reports a history of low-grade fever for the past 3 weeks and a persistent, dry cough. There is no known history of contact with a person diagnosed with tuberculosis, but the father works in a nearby city and frequently returns home. On examination, the child appears lethargic, with a respiratory rate of 38/min and no audible wheeze or crackles. There is bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy with firm, non-tender nodes measuring approximately 1.5 cm each. A chest radiograph reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without consolidation. A tuberculin skin test (TST) performed on day 3 of admission is negative. The child’s nutritional feeds have been verified, and acute infections such as pneumonia and gastroenteritis have been ruled out. The medical team considers further evaluation for tuberculosis and coordination with the NTEP diagnostic network.
case_studies.learning_objective: To assess and manage a child with severe acute malnutrition who is non-responsive to therapeutic feeding, with a focus on tuberculosis screening and linkage to the National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP).

subtopics.subtopic_title: EXTENSIVE SKIN INFECTION MANAGEMENT AND ANTISTAPHYLOCOCCAL COVERAGE
subtopics.concept: Extensive skin breakdown in oedematous severe acute malnutrition (SAM) poses a significant risk for systemic infection, including bacteremia, even in afebrile children. Conditions such as weeping kwashiorkor dermatosis, ulceration, spreading cellulitis, or abscesses should be treated as systemic infection risks, necessitating early reassessment of antibiotic coverage. For admitted or complicated cases of SAM, initial parenteral antibiotic therapy typically includes ampicillin plus gentamicin. Anti-staphylococcal coverage, such as cloxacillin, should be added when skin lesions are extensive, purulent, rapidly spreading, or associated with post-measles pneumonia, as staphylococcal infections are common in these scenarios. Antibiotic therapy should be escalated at 48–72 hours if there is clinical deterioration or no improvement, guided by local antibiograms and culture results when available. Effective wound care is critical and must be explicitly prescribed. Skin should be handled gently, avoiding adhesive tapes on fragile areas, while ensuring bedding remains soft and dry to prevent pressure injuries. Frequent repositioning is essential to reduce the risk of pressure ulcers. Lesions should be cleaned with gentle soap-and-water or protocol-appropriate soaks, followed by the application of barrier dressings such as petrolatum or zinc-based formulations to minimize fluid loss and bacterial entry. Caustic topical antiseptics should be avoided as they may delay healing. Open lesions increase insensible fluid losses, so monitoring for dehydration and hypothermia is vital. Adequate analgesia should be provided to support oral intake and sleep, and scabies or fungal involvement should be treated when suspected. Daily skin assessments must be documented as part of the infection reassessment process in complicated SAM cases. These measures help mitigate the risk of systemic infection and optimize recovery in children with extensive skin infections.

subtopics.subtopic_title: POTASSIUM MAGNESIUM AND SODIUM RESTRICTION IN EARLY SAM CARE
subtopics.concept: In the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM), early correction of potassium, magnesium, and sodium imbalances is a critical, lifesaving intervention. Total-body deficits in potassium and magnesium contribute to muscle weakness, arrhythmias, and nutritional oedema, even when serum levels appear normal. Repletion of these electrolytes should begin on day 0, with strict sodium restriction and avoidance of diuretics for nutritional oedema. Potassium supplementation is typically targeted at 3–4 mEq/kg/day for a minimum of two weeks, administered in divided doses with feeds or diluted into therapeutic milk. Dosage adjustments are essential if renal failure or anuria is suspected. Magnesium repletion starts with a single intramuscular dose of 50% magnesium sulfate at 0.3 mL/kg (maximum 2 mL) on day 1, followed by oral magnesium at 0.4–0.6 mmol/kg/day for approximately two weeks. If oral preparations are unavailable, small, measured volumes of magnesium sulfate can be mixed into feeds according to facility protocols. Sodium restriction must be strictly enforced using “no added salt” feeds, avoiding high-sodium family foods, and refraining from the use of standard high-sodium intravenous fluids, except in carefully managed shock protocols. During early management, weight changes in oedematous SAM must be interpreted cautiously, as initial weight loss may reflect resolution of oedema rather than true nutritional improvement. Overhydration and cardiac intolerance should be vigilantly monitored during rehydration and feed transitions, with warning signs including rising respiratory rate, increasing pulse, new hepatomegaly, or periorbital puffiness. Where laboratory monitoring is available, phosphate levels should be checked during transition feeding, as hypophosphatemia may indicate refeeding syndrome. This requires immediate electrolyte correction and a slower advancement of feeds. These measures are essential to address the metabolic derangements in SAM and to optimize outcomes in paediatric patients.

case_studies.title: Electrolyte Imbalances in Early SAM Management
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India is brought to the emergency department with lethargy, poor feeding, and worsening swelling of his feet over the past 5 days. His mother reports he had frequent watery stools two weeks ago, followed by decreased appetite and irritability. On examination, the child appears apathetic, with bilateral pitting oedema on the lower limbs, a distended abdomen, and visible wasting of the upper arms. His weight-for-height is markedly low, and his mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) measures 10.5 cm. Vitals reveal a pulse of 120/min and respiratory rate of 35/min, with no fever. Auscultation shows normal heart sounds, and there is no hepatomegaly. The child is alert but weak, unable to sit unsupported. Laboratory tests are unavailable in the current setting. The family mentions difficulty accessing clean water and nutritious food. The healthcare team notes the need for close monitoring during nutritional rehabilitation, particularly for fluid balance and electrolyte correction.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the importance of potassium, magnesium, and sodium management in early SAM care and identify warning signs of overhydration or cardiac intolerance during nutritional rehabilitation.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MICRONUTRIENT SUPPLEMENTATION SCHEDULE IN INPATIENT SAM CARE
subtopics.concept: In the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in paediatric inpatients, micronutrient supplementation must be carefully tailored to the child’s nutritional intake. If therapeutic foods such as F-75, F-100, or ready-to-use therapeutic foods (RUTF) are correctly prepared and meet prescribed specifications, additional multivitamins and trace elements are generally unnecessary and may lead to duplication. However, when local milk-sugar-oil recipes or home-prepared foods are used, supplementation must be explicit, reliable, and well-documented. A standard inpatient supplementation schedule, aligned with Indian facility protocols, should be followed during the stabilization and early rehabilitation phases. On day one, provide folic acid at a dose of 5 mg, followed by 1 mg daily thereafter. Zinc supplementation, typically at 2 mg/kg/day, and copper, at 0.3 mg/kg/day, should be continued for at least two weeks. Additionally, a daily multivitamin preparation containing vitamin B complex, vitamin C, and vitamin D should be administered as per the local formulary. To enhance tolerability and compliance, oral micronutrients should be given with feeds, and nursing intake charts must include a medication checklist to ensure doses are not missed, particularly during night shifts. In cases where vomiting or severe diarrhoea limits oral intake, priority should be given to stabilization feeding and infection management, with micronutrient supplementation reintroduced once tolerance improves. It is crucial to differentiate between routine supplementation schedules and condition-triggered therapies. For example, iron and high-dose vitamin A have specific timing and indications in SAM management and should be prescribed as separate, dedicated orders rather than being included in generic multivitamin regimens to ensure safety and appropriate use. This structured approach to micronutrient therapy is essential for optimizing outcomes in paediatric SAM care.

case_studies.title: Micronutrient Supplementation in Inpatient SAM Care
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy is admitted to a district hospital in rural India for severe acute malnutrition. His mother reports a history of persistent diarrhea for the past 3 weeks, poor appetite, and weight loss. Examination reveals bilateral pedal edema, visible wasting, and a weight-for-height z-score of -4. He appears lethargic and irritable. His vital signs are stable, with no fever. The hospital lacks access to commercial therapeutic foods like F-75 or F-100, and the staff relies on locally prepared milk-sugar-oil feeds for stabilization. On day two of admission, the nurse notes that the child vomited twice after feeds and refused food during the night shift. Intake charts reveal inconsistent documentation of micronutrient administration. The attending physician is concerned about micronutrient deficiencies and considers whether supplementation adjustments are needed to prevent complications during the stabilization phase.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the importance of a standardized micronutrient supplementation schedule in inpatient SAM care, particularly when therapeutic foods are unavailable or tolerance is impaired.

subtopics.subtopic_title: IRON INITIATION CRITERIA AND DOSING DURING REHABILITATION
subtopics.concept: Iron supplementation during the rehabilitation phase of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is carefully timed to avoid adverse effects associated with early initiation. Early iron supplementation during the stabilization phase is contraindicated due to the risk of increased oxidative stress and worsened outcomes in the presence of uncontrolled infections. Iron should only be introduced once the child is clinically stable and showing clear signs of improvement, such as the return of appetite, absence of recurrent hypothermia or hypoglycemia, reduction or resolution of edema, and improvement in acute infection. This typically corresponds to approximately day 7 of inpatient care in many protocols, although local guidelines may allow earlier initiation if the child is confirmed to be in the rehabilitation phase and infection is controlled. The recommended dose of elemental iron is approximately 3 mg/kg/day, as per the local formulary, and it should be continued throughout the rehabilitation phase with appropriate follow-up planning. Iron supplementation should be paired with ongoing folate and nutritional rehabilitation, and the dose should not be escalated to hasten hemoglobin recovery. In cases of severe anemia, blood transfusion is preferred over early iron supplementation. Transfusion is indicated when hemoglobin is less than 4 g/dL or when hemoglobin is between 4–6 g/dL with respiratory distress, and this should be managed cautiously with close monitoring as per facility protocols. Persistent poor weight gain or failure of anemia to improve should prompt reassessment for underlying issues such as ongoing infection, chronic inflammation, parasitosis, or hemoglobinopathy, and verification of therapeutic feeding and micronutrient delivery. Additional iron should not be prescribed if the child is receiving correctly formulated ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) unless a separate clinical indication exists, as duplication can increase adverse effects and reduce adherence.

subtopics.subtopic_title: VITAMIN A SUPPLEMENTATION INDICATIONS IN SAM
subtopics.concept: Vitamin A supplementation in the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) should be guided by specific indications, particularly the presence of eye signs or measles risk. Routine high-dose vitamin A administration must be approached cautiously to avoid duplication when fortified therapeutic foods are used, as repeated megadosing can lead to toxicity. Conversely, missed dosing in cases of measles or corneal disease can result in blindness. At admission, children with SAM should be assessed for conjunctival xerosis, Bitot spots, corneal clouding or ulceration, and a history of recent high-dose vitamin A intake. Any corneal involvement warrants immediate ophthalmic care and urgent vitamin A therapy. High-dose vitamin A should be administered on day 1 if there are clinical signs of vitamin A deficiency or if measles is suspected or confirmed. The recommended doses are 50,000 IU for children under 6 months, 100,000 IU for those aged 6–12 months, and 200,000 IU for those older than 12 months, following standard pediatric protocols. Local or national guidelines should be consulted for repeat dosing schedules, which often involve administration after 24 hours and again after 2 weeks in cases of frank xerophthalmia. However, repeat dosing should be avoided if a high dose was given within the preceding month unless specifically indicated. In edematous SAM with dermatosis, vitamin A status must be actively evaluated due to compromised skin and mucosal integrity. For children with complicated measles or pneumonia, vitamin A supplementation should not be delayed while awaiting laboratory confirmation. Upon discharge, it is essential to document whether a high dose of vitamin A was administered and communicate this information to the outpatient team to prevent duplicate dosing. It is important to distinguish vitamin A therapy, which is aimed at preventing complications, from measles vaccination, which serves as a preventive measure once the child is clinically stable.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DEWORMING TIMING AND ANTIHELMINTHIC DOSES DURING REHABILITATION
subtopics.concept: Deworming in the context of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is a rehabilitation-phase intervention and should not be initiated during the initial stabilization period. Early inpatient care must prioritize addressing hypoglycemia, hypothermia, infections, and careful feeding, as these are critical to reducing early mortality. Administering anthelmintics during this phase may exacerbate vomiting in unstable children and does not address the immediate metabolic vulnerabilities that contribute to early deaths. Deworming should be planned only after the child is clinically stable, has transitioned to catch-up feeding, and can reliably tolerate oral medications. This typically occurs after the first 48–72 hours of stabilization or at the time of discharge. The recommended age-specific dosing per Indian protocols includes albendazole 200 mg as a single dose for children aged 12–23 months and 400 mg as a single dose for those aged 24 months and older. In cases where albendazole is unavailable or local guidelines prefer, mebendazole 100 mg twice daily for three days can be used for children aged 24 months and older. Deworming is not recommended for children under 12 months unless explicitly directed by a specific program policy. In children with persistent diarrhea, empiric deworming should not replace stool examination for giardiasis or other pathogens. Deworming should be integrated into the outpatient follow-up workflow, with documentation of the dose and date administered. It is essential to pair deworming with caregiver counseling on household hygiene practices, such as handwashing, safe disposal of child feces, and the use of footwear where feasible, to minimize the risk of reinfection. Caregivers should be informed that mild, transient abdominal discomfort may occur after deworming, but persistent vomiting, abdominal distension, rash, or worsening anorexia should prompt reassessment rather than repeating the dose. In regions with a high burden of anemia, it is important not to attribute anemia solely to helminth infections; further evaluation for dietary deficiencies, infections, and severe anemia requiring transfusion should be conducted in accordance with SAM management protocols.

subtopics.subtopic_title: WEIGHT-BASED F-75 PRESCRIPTION AND FEED VOLUME TARGETS
subtopics.concept: In the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM), weight-based F-75 prescription and feed volume targets are critical for metabolic stabilization and the prevention of hypoglycemia during the initial stabilization phase. F-75, a low-sodium, low-protein therapeutic diet, is prescribed in precise, executable bedside terms such as “F-75 ___ mL per feed every ___ hours; daily target ___ mL; minimum acceptable intake (80% of target) ___ mL/day; if intake <80% initiate NG top-up.” The total fluid target is approximately 130 mL/kg/day, though this should be reduced to about 100 mL/kg/day in cases of severe (+++) edema. Feeds are initiated every two hours for at least the first day and transitioned to three-hourly or four-hourly intervals only when vomiting and diarrhea are controlled, and the child consistently finishes feeds. Feeding progression must adhere to explicit rules, avoiding volume increases based on perceived hunger on day one. Prescribed volumes and frequencies should be maintained until the child’s temperature stabilizes, hypoglycemia resolves, and appetite improves. In cases of vomiting, the feed volume per session should be reduced, and the frequency increased rather than skipping feeds. Breastfeeding should continue on demand alongside therapeutic feeds. Each feed must be documented, including the volume offered, volume consumed, and any leftovers. If two consecutive feeds are ≤80% of the prescribed volume despite assisted feeding, this should trigger nasogastric (NG) tube completion and reassessment for potential complications such as sepsis, electrolyte imbalances, or intestinal pathology. It is important to note that ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) should not be administered via an NG tube; liquid therapeutic feeds are recommended for tube delivery. These protocols ensure safe and effective feeding practices during the stabilization phase of SAM management.

case_studies.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Initiating F-75 Feeds
case_studies.vignette:
A 14-month-old boy weighing 5.2 kg is brought to a district hospital in rural India by his mother with complaints of lethargy, poor feeding, and loose stools for the past 3 days. The child is visibly emaciated, with marked wasting, sunken eyes, and bilateral pedal edema (++). On examination, his temperature is 35.7°C, heart rate is 128 bpm, and he appears drowsy but arousable. The mother reports that he has refused food and breastmilk for the past day, and he has vomited twice in the last 12 hours. The child has passed three watery stools today. A rapid blood glucose test shows 48 mg/dL. 

The paediatric ward is equipped for therapeutic feeding, and the child is admitted for further care. The medical team plans to initiate therapeutic feeds. The nurse inquires about the prescribed F-75 feed volume and frequency, considering the child’s weight, presence of edema, and risk of hypoglycemia. Documentation of feed intake and any vomiting is emphasized during the initial stabilization phase.
case_studies.learning_objective: Apply weight-based F-75 prescription and feed volume targets in the initial stabilization phase of SAM, considering edema, hypoglycemia, and feeding tolerance.

subtopics.subtopic_title: F-75 AND F-100 PREPARATION WITH ELECTROLYTE FORTIFICATION
subtopics.concept: The preparation of F-75 and F-100 therapeutic feeds with electrolyte fortification is a critical component of managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in paediatric patients. Therapeutic feeding safety hinges on accurate preparation and appropriate mineral composition, beyond merely achieving caloric targets. When premixed F-75 or F-100 formulations are unavailable, locally prepared recipes must reliably incorporate electrolytes and micronutrients. Common facility-based recipes in India include a starter diet (F-75 equivalent) made from milk, sugar, oil, and water, and a catch-up diet (F-100 equivalent) with a higher milk content alongside added sugar and oil. All feeds should be prepared with clean utensils, boiled or safe water, and clearly labeled with the time of preparation. Feeds must be discarded if stored beyond the protocol-defined window to minimize bacterial contamination risks. Electrolyte fortification is essential for addressing the specific needs of severely malnourished children. These patients require added potassium and magnesium while restricting sodium intake. If an electrolyte or mineral solution is available, it should be mixed into the therapeutic milk as per the manufacturer’s instructions to ensure routine supplementation in each feed. In its absence, potassium and magnesium must be prescribed separately, with dosing documented meticulously, as delays in supplementation can lead to arrhythmias and feeding failure. Practical measures at the bedside include double-checking feed volume calculations against the child’s weight, verifying that oil and sugar quantities match the prescribed recipe—since under-measurement can result in poor weight gain—and ensuring feeds are warmed before administration. Night feeds must be delivered as scheduled. If diarrhoea worsens following a change in feed preparation, it should initially be treated as a potential hygiene or mixing-process issue. Caregivers should be retrained on proper preparation techniques before discharge to prevent recurrence. These steps are vital to ensure the efficacy and safety of therapeutic feeding in children with SAM.

subtopics.subtopic_title: FEEDING INTOLERANCE TROUBLESHOOTING AND NURSING DOCUMENTATION
subtopics.concept: Feeding intolerance in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) necessitates protocol adjustments and diagnostic reassessment rather than cessation of feeds. The primary goal in the ward is to sustain frequent small feeds, avoid fasting, and identify the underlying cause of poor tolerance, which may include incorrect feed volumes, cold feeds, sepsis, electrolyte imbalances, or evolving heart failure. Vomiting within the first 48 hours should prompt a reduction in feed volume, a return to 2-hourly feeding intervals, and the use of warm feeds. Persistent vomiting warrants rechecking glucose and temperature and investigating for possible infection or obstruction. It is crucial to avoid "pushing through" by increasing feed volumes or transitioning prematurely to catch-up feeding. For persistent watery diarrhea during stabilization, breastfeeding and F-75 feeds should be continued, with fluid losses replaced between feeds using malnutrition-appropriate oral or nasogastric (NG) rehydration solutions. Frequent reassessment is necessary, and if dehydration is suspected, fluids should be administered slowly while monitoring for signs of overhydration, such as rising respiratory rate, increased pulse, periorbital puffiness, or worsening edema. Signs such as abdominal distension, new-onset tachypnea, or a rising pulse during feeding may indicate fluid overload or early heart failure. In such cases, feeds should be reduced, additional fluids stopped, and liver size, perfusion, sodium exposures, and feed preparation reviewed. Nursing documentation plays a critical role in ensuring safety and should include temperature, pulse, respiratory rate trends, feed volumes offered and consumed, episodes of vomiting and diarrhea, urine output proxy, edema grade, and any signs of overhydration. Escalation triggers, such as intake of less than 80% of the prescribed feeds over 24 hours, recurrent hypoglycemia, or new respiratory distress, must be promptly acted upon rather than merely recorded. These measures ensure timely identification and management of feeding intolerance in children with SAM, supporting their recovery and minimizing complications.

subtopics.subtopic_title: NASOGASTRIC FEEDING INDICATIONS PLACEMENT CONFIRMATION AND ASPIRATION PREVENTION
subtopics.concept:
Nasogastric (NG) feeding is an essential intervention in the inpatient management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) to ensure adequate nutritional intake when appetite or feeding endurance is compromised. It should be initiated early when oral intake is unreliable to prevent repeated missed feeds and recurrent hypoglycemia. Specific indications for NG feeding include the inability to consume prescribed F-75 volumes, persistent intake of less than 80% of the target despite assisted feeding, weak suck or unsafe swallow, and the need to administer glucose or feeds when the child cannot drink. However, NG feeding is contraindicated in cases of depressed consciousness with poor airway protection, suspected upper gastrointestinal obstruction, severe facial trauma, and uncontrolled vomiting, all of which require senior clinical decision-making. 

Proper placement of the NG tube must be confirmed before its first use. The insertion length should be measured using the standard nose-to-earlobe-to-mid-umbilicus method, and the external length should be marked. Gastric aspirate pH testing is recommended when available, with feeding considered safe if the aspirate pH is ≤5.5. If no aspirate is obtained or the pH is higher, the tube should not be used until radiographic confirmation is obtained or the tube is re-sited according to pediatric safety guidelines. Position checks are necessary after events such as vomiting, violent retching, or suspected displacement. 

To prevent aspiration during NG feeding, practical bedside measures include keeping the child’s head elevated during feeding, avoiding supine positioning, delivering feeds slowly via bolus, and pausing feeds during episodes of significant respiratory distress. Weaning from NG feeding should be planned when the child reliably consumes at least 80% of the target intake orally over 24 hours without vomiting and exhibits stable respiratory status. These steps ensure safe and effective nutritional support for children with SAM.

case_studies.title: Nasogastric Feeding in Severe Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 15-month-old boy from a rural village in India is admitted to the pediatric ward with severe acute malnutrition. He has a weight-for-height Z-score of -4 and visible wasting. His mother reports that he has been lethargic and refusing to eat for the past three days. On examination, he is irritable, has a weak cry, and demonstrates poor eye contact. His respiratory rate is 38/min, and he has no signs of respiratory distress. Assisted feeding with F-75 formula is attempted, but he consumes less than 50% of the prescribed volume and shows signs of fatigue with a weak suck. The decision is made to initiate nasogastric feeding. A nurse inserts the NG tube using the nose-to-earlobe-to-mid-umbilicus measurement and marks the external length. Gastric aspirate is obtained, and the pH is tested, showing a value of 5.0. The child is placed in a semi-upright position during feeding, and bolus feeds are delivered slowly. The team monitors for signs of aspiration and ensures proper positioning throughout. After a vomiting episode, the tube’s placement is rechecked before resuming feeds.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the indications for nasogastric feeding in severe acute malnutrition, methods for confirming placement, and strategies to prevent aspiration during feeding.

subtopics.subtopic_title: TRANSITION TO F-100 CATCH-UP GROWTH AND REFEEDING SAFETY
subtopics.concept: Transitioning from stabilization feeding to rehabilitation feeding in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is a critical and carefully monitored process to prevent complications such as heart failure. Rehabilitation feeding with F-100 should only commence when the child demonstrates clinical readiness, including the return of appetite, stabilization of temperature, absence of hypoglycemia, and reduction or resolution of oedema. The transition from F-75 to F-100 follows a structured protocol, beginning with replacing F-75 with an equal volume of F-100 for approximately two days. Feed volumes are then incrementally increased until small amounts remain uneaten, which serves as a bedside indicator of ad libitum tolerance. Frequent feeding, typically eight feeds over 24 hours, is maintained during this phase, and careful monitoring for signs of cardiac intolerance—such as tachypnea, rising pulse, hepatomegaly, or recurrent oedema—is essential. Catch-up growth during rehabilitation is both an outcome measure and a safety signal. Daily weight gain is categorized as good at ≥10 g/kg/day, moderate at 5 to <10 g/kg/day, and poor at <5 g/kg/day. Early weight loss in oedematous SAM should be interpreted cautiously, as it may reflect oedema resolution rather than inadequate nutritional intake. If weight gain is poor despite verified feed delivery, clinicians should reassess for potential causes such as sharing of feeds, missed night feeds, persistent diarrhea, malabsorption, or chronic infections like tuberculosis. Refeeding syndrome is a serious risk during rapid nutritional rehabilitation, characterized by intracellular shifts of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, which can lead to arrhythmias, weakness, and respiratory failure. Prevention involves adherence to phased feeding protocols (F-75 followed by F-100 or ready-to-use therapeutic food [RUTF]), early repletion of potassium and magnesium, and monitoring of electrolytes, including phosphate, when available. If refeeding syndrome is suspected, feed advancement should be slowed, and urgent correction of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium levels should be undertaken while closely monitoring cardiorespiratory status.

case_studies.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Transition to F-100 and Monitoring Safety
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India was admitted to the pediatric ward with severe acute malnutrition (weight-for-height Z-score <-3 and bilateral pitting edema). After 5 days of stabilization with F-75, his appetite improved, hypoglycemia resolved, and his oedema reduced significantly. On transitioning to F-100, his feeding schedule was maintained at eight feeds per day, with an initial volume matching his previous intake of F-75. Two days later, the staff noted mild tachypnea (respiratory rate 46/min) and a rising pulse rate from 110/min to 130/min over 24 hours. He appeared irritable, and hepatomegaly was detected on focused examination. His daily weight chart showed a gain of 3 g/kg/day, but oedema seemed to persist slightly. The mother reported difficulty completing nighttime feeds due to her exhaustion. Laboratory testing was unavailable at the facility. The team was concerned about refeeding safety and cardiac intolerance during the rehabilitation phase.

subtopics.subtopic_title: REFEEDING SYNDROME RECOGNITION THIAMINE AND PHOSPHATE REPLACEMENT
subtopics.concept: Refeeding syndrome is a predictable risk in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) due to depleted potassium, magnesium, and phosphate stores. The carbohydrate-driven insulin release following feeding shifts these electrolytes intracellularly, potentially leading to clinical deterioration within 2–5 days of advancing feeds, even when calorie intake seems appropriate. Refeeding syndrome should be suspected if a child develops new tachypnea or hypoxia without another explanation, arrhythmia or sudden tachycardia, worsening neuromuscular weakness or lethargy, seizures, or increased edema during feed advancement. Laboratory findings, where available, may show falling levels of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, which are key markers of this condition. In managing suspected refeeding syndrome, feeding should not be completely stopped unless airway protection is compromised. Instead, caloric advancement should be temporarily reduced, reverting toward stabilization volumes, and feed frequency increased. Electrolytes must be corrected proactively, with routine supplementation of potassium (approximately 3–4 mmol/kg/day) and magnesium (approximately 0.4–0.6 mmol/kg/day) continuing even without laboratory confirmation. Thiamine should be administered before further feed escalation, where feasible. Concurrent infections should be treated aggressively, as sepsis and refeeding syndrome often coexist. Phosphate replacement is specifically indicated in cases of moderate-to-severe hypophosphatemia or when symptoms are present. Enteral phosphate is preferred when gut function is intact and aspiration risk is low, with dosing divided as per pediatric monographs. Severe or symptomatic hypophosphatemia necessitates intravenous phosphate replacement, accompanied by cardiac and calcium monitoring. Dose adjustments are required in cases of renal dysfunction. If phosphate products or testing are unavailable, feeding advancement should be slowed, and urgent referral arranged for electrolyte correction. Recognizing and addressing refeeding syndrome promptly is critical to improving outcomes in children with SAM.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SENSORY STIMULATION AND EMOTIONAL SUPPORT DURING REHABILITATION
subtopics.concept: Sensory stimulation and emotional support play a critical role in the rehabilitation of children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and should be treated as prescribed interventions once the child is clinically stable. Developmental and psychosocial care are essential to address apathy, irritability, and caregiver distress, which can negatively impact nutritional intake, increase defaulting, and delay neurodevelopmental recovery despite technically correct therapeutic feeding. A structured daily routine is vital during ward-based care, including encouraging caregivers to remain with the child, promoting responsive feeding characterized by calm and unhurried interactions without force, and scheduling short play sessions lasting 15–30 minutes at least twice daily. These sessions should incorporate age-appropriate, low-cost toys and interactive activities such as talking, singing, and simple games. Safe mobility and exposure to sunshine should be encouraged when the child is stable, and group play can be introduced where feasible to reduce isolation and enhance caregiver skill transfer. Improvements in engagement and playfulness should be documented alongside intake and weight gain, as enhanced alertness and playfulness often serve as early markers of recovery. It is important to identify barriers that may predict relapse, such as maternal depression, household food insecurity, domestic violence, or feeding problems related to disabilities, which may require tailored counseling or referral. For children experiencing prolonged anorexia, vomiting, or suspected oral pain, healthcare providers should assess for conditions such as thrush, dental issues, or painful skin lesions and address pain management to restore nutritional intake. Before discharge or transitioning to outpatient care under Community-Based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM), families should be provided with a simple home stimulation plan. Caregivers must be counseled to monitor the child’s appetite and interaction, as deterioration in activity is a danger sign even if weight has not yet fallen. These measures ensure that emotional and sensory interventions complement nutritional rehabilitation and support holistic recovery.

subtopics.subtopic_title: RUTF SPECIFICATIONS CAREGIVER COUNSELING AND SACHET DOSING
subtopics.concept: Ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) is an energy-dense, micronutrient-fortified paste primarily used for outpatient management of uncomplicated severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in children older than six months. It is a specialized medical nutrition therapy and should not be confused with general complementary foods. RUTF must not be administered via a nasogastric (NG) tube; liquid therapeutic formulas are recommended for inpatient NG feeding. Caregiver counseling is essential to ensure the safe and effective use of RUTF. Each sachet provides 500 kcal, and caregivers should divide the prescribed daily sachets into multiple small feeds, offer clean water after each feed, and continue breastfeeding on demand. Sharing RUTF within the household is strictly discouraged, and opened sachets must be discarded after 24 hours. Caregivers should be educated on “return today” danger signs, which include new fever, fast breathing, convulsions, lethargy, repeated vomiting that prevents intake, new or worsening oedema, or inability to consume the prescribed RUTF. Weekly follow-up visits are recommended for re-measurement and resupply. Energy targets for RUTF are based on the 2023 WHO dosing framework, which recommends 150–185 kcal/kg/day until full recovery or until the child is no longer severely wasted and has no nutritional oedema, followed by 100–130 kcal/kg/day until recovery. Practical sachet dosing is determined by weight, with examples including 1.5 sachets/day for children weighing 4.0–5.0 kg, 2.0 sachets/day for those weighing 5.5–6.7 kg, and 3.0 sachets/day for weights of 8.4–10.0 kg. Exact kcal/kg matching is not feasible due to discrete sachet sizes, and dose reduction is unsafe when household food security or follow-up is unreliable. Adjustments to dosing should only be made within established protocols to ensure safety and efficacy.

subtopics.subtopic_title: THERAPEUTIC FOOD AVAILABILITY STOCK-OUT ADAPTATIONS AND VISIT WORKFLOW
subtopics.concept: Therapeutic food availability in India for the outpatient management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is highly variable, as there is no universally adopted product across states. Community-based management of acute malnutrition (CMAM) relies on access to therapeutic foods, but districts may use ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF), locally produced equivalents, or state-designed supplements. Inpatient care typically depends on locally prepared F-75 and F-100 recipes as per Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW) guidelines. For paediatric clinicians, the critical priority is adherence to protocols rather than reliance on specific brands. This includes accurate triage and disposition, cautious medical management (fluids, antibiotics, electrolytes), adequate energy/protein/micronutrient delivery, and strict follow-up with clear escalation criteria. In situations where therapeutic food stock-outs occur, predefined adaptation protocols are essential to avoid ad hoc decision-making. According to the 2023 WHO framework, structured dose reduction should only be considered after a child no longer meets SAM criteria, has no nutritional oedema, and has access to reliable complementary foods and follow-up care. A standardized outpatient CMAM visit should emulate a brief but systematic ward round. At every visit, clinicians must screen for danger signs and assess for new or worsening oedema to determine the need for immediate referral. Appetite should be verified alongside practical feeding feasibility, and weight should be measured to adjust dosing while tracking mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) or weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) responses when available. Additionally, household risks such as food sharing, missed appointments, stock-outs, travel barriers, and intercurrent illnesses should be evaluated. At the initial visit, a focused clinical examination and a standardized appetite test using the therapeutic food intended for home use are mandatory. Caregiver demonstration of feeding practices and hygiene counseling should be initiated, and follow-up must be scheduled at least weekly until recovery progress is evident. These measures ensure continuity of care and mitigate risks associated with therapeutic food variability or stock-outs.

subtopics.subtopic_title: OUTPATIENT ESCALATION DEFAULTING AND DISCORDANT ANTHROPOMETRY DECISIONS
subtopics.concept: Outpatient management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) requires clearly defined escalation criteria, as deterioration can occur rapidly and infection signs are often muted in these children. Immediate referral for inpatient stabilization is warranted if any new danger signs develop, the appetite test fails, oedema appears or worsens, repeated vomiting prevents intake, severe dehydration or shock is suspected, or if caregivers are unable to safely administer therapeutic feeds. Persistent anorexia, despite initial outpatient antibiotic treatment, should prompt reassessment for missed infections or errors in feeding delivery rather than continuation of the same plan. Defaulting and food-sharing are common challenges in outpatient programs and should not be viewed as moral failings. Large-scale programs in India have demonstrated higher default rates in areas with difficult travel or weak community referral systems, while referrals by frontline workers, such as Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs), significantly reduce default risks. Incorporating simple adherence questions during every visit—such as “Who else eats the therapeutic food?”, “Were any days missed?”, “Were there any stockouts?”, and “Can you return next week?”—can help identify barriers and guide intensified counseling and follow-up. Discordant anthropometry must be explicitly addressed during both enrollment and discharge. Since mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) and weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) do not always identify the same at-risk children, enrollment should occur if either measure meets the SAM threshold or if nutritional oedema is present. For discharge, a single-marker approach is unsafe; children should not be discharged solely because MUAC has normalized if WHZ remains below -3, nor based on WHZ improvement alone if MUAC remains severely low. A safe discharge protocol includes documenting both MUAC and WHZ at baseline and at planned discharge, ensuring the absence of nutritional oedema on repeated visits, and confirming sustained clinical improvement and consistent weight gain alongside anthropometric recovery. These measures are critical to ensuring safe and effective outpatient SAM management.

case_studies.title: Outpatient Defaulting and Discordant Anthropometry in SAM
case_studies.vignette: A 13-month-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the outpatient therapeutic program for severe acute malnutrition (SAM) after missing his last two follow-up visits. He was enrolled three weeks ago with a mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of 10.8 cm and a weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) of -3.5. His mother reports that he has been refusing the therapeutic food for the past week, often spitting it out. She admits that some of the food was shared with his siblings due to household food scarcity. Over the last four days, he has developed reduced activity, intermittent vomiting, and persistent fever. On examination, he appears lethargic, with dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, and mild bilateral pitting oedema. His weight has not changed since enrollment. MUAC is now 10.7 cm, but WHZ remains below -3. The local Accredited Social Health Activist (ASHA) who referred him expresses concern about the family’s ability to continue outpatient care due to long travel distances and financial constraints.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize escalation criteria for inpatient stabilization in SAM, including danger signs, persistent anorexia, and discordant anthropometry, while addressing barriers like defaulting and food-sharing.

subtopics.subtopic_title: OUTPATIENT REFERRAL AND ESCALATION CRITERIA IN CMAM
subtopics.concept: Outpatient management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) within the Community-Based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) framework in India is deemed safe only when clear escalation criteria are adhered to, ensuring rapid referral upon signs of deterioration. Admission and escalation criteria include immediate inpatient referral for any danger signs such as lethargy, convulsions, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, or respiratory distress. Additional triggers for escalation include failure of the appetite test, onset or worsening of bilateral pitting edema, or repeated vomiting that impedes oral intake. Suspected severe dehydration or shock should always be managed as an inpatient condition, as the risks of overhydration and diagnostic uncertainty are heightened in SAM cases. Furthermore, lack of recovery momentum, such as persistent appetite loss despite appropriate outpatient antibiotics and verified feeding, necessitates clinical reassessment to identify potential missed infections, errors in therapeutic food delivery, or caregiver-related barriers. High-risk non-response indicators, including weight loss between visits, new edema development, or failure to achieve weight gain over two successive visits, also warrant immediate referral for inpatient care. Initial laboratory investigations are not specified in the source. Stabilization steps are similarly not detailed in the source. Thresholds for admission to the pediatric intensive care unit (PICU) are not specified in the source. However, practical "social" triggers are highlighted as equally critical medical considerations. If caregivers are unable to demonstrate safe and frequent feeding practices, fail to return reliably for at least weekly follow-ups, report therapeutic food stock-outs or sharing that cannot be resolved, or lack access to safe water and sanitation, inpatient initiation or supervised bridging care should be prioritized over continued outpatient management. Discharge and review triggers are not specified in the source. These guidelines underscore the importance of vigilant monitoring and timely escalation to prevent adverse outcomes in children with SAM managed under outpatient CMAM protocols.

case_studies.title: Outpatient Referral and Escalation in SAM Management
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy from a rural village in India presents to the local CMAM outpatient center for follow-up after being diagnosed with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) two weeks ago. He initially passed the appetite test and was prescribed ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) alongside outpatient antibiotics for a suspected respiratory infection. His mother reports he has been lethargic for the past two days and is refusing to eat. She also mentions that the child has vomited multiple times since yesterday. On examination, the boy appears drowsy and irritable when stimulated. His weight has decreased by 200 grams since the last visit, and bilateral pitting edema is noted on his feet, which was not present previously. His respiratory rate is 48 breaths per minute, and he has a weak radial pulse. The caregiver admits difficulty in feeding due to vomiting and reports that the RUTF supply ran out two days ago.

case_studies.title: Outpatient Referral and Escalation in Severe Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old girl from a rural village in India, weighing 5.2 kg, has been enrolled in an outpatient CMAM program for severe acute malnutrition for the past three weeks. She initially showed modest weight gain but has now experienced weight loss over the last two visits. Her mother reports that the child has been refusing the ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) for the past five days and has had three episodes of vomiting daily. On examination, the child appears lethargic and irritable, with bilateral pitting edema extending to her knees. Her temperature is 36.0°C, and she has a respiratory rate of 46 breaths per minute with mild intercostal retractions. The mother reports no recent fever or diarrhea but admits difficulty in adhering to feeding instructions due to competing household responsibilities. The family lives over 15 km from the nearest health facility, and transportation is unreliable. No other children in the household appear to be ill.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify clinical and social escalation criteria for inpatient referral in the outpatient management of severe acute malnutrition under the CMAM framework.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MANAGING DEFAULTING AND ADHERENCE FAILURES IN CMAM
subtopics.concept: Managing defaulting and adherence failures in Community-Based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) programs for paediatric patients requires a structured approach to ensure timely intervention and prevent clinical deterioration. Expected defervescence timelines are not specified in the source. Treatment failure is suspected when there is failure to gain weight, clinical deterioration, danger signs, weight loss, or edema relapse. In these cases, urgent facility referral should be prioritized rather than waiting for the next consignment during supply interruptions. A stepwise work-up involves tracing missed visits and addressing adherence barriers. After one missed visit, same-week outreach by ASHA/AWW through home visits or phone contact should be triggered, with rebooking within 48–72 hours. After two missed visits, escalation to supervisor/ANM review is necessary to assess for illness or social barriers. If danger signs, weight loss, or edema are suspected, immediate referral to a facility is warranted. Community platforms such as Village Health, Sanitation, and Nutrition Days (VHSND) or automated messaging systems can be utilized to reinforce return visits. Documentation of tracing attempts and outcomes is essential to reduce “silent defaults.” Stock-outs require a pre-defined playbook, including routine stock reconciliation, early warning systems, redistribution between centers when feasible, and protocol-approved temporary bridging only when follow-up is reliable. Therapy adjustments, such as switching or extending treatment, are not specified in the source. Follow-up measures include asking concrete adherence questions during each visit, such as whether any doses of therapeutic food were missed, whether others consumed the food, any vomiting or refusal, stock-outs, and the ability to return the following week. Identified barriers should be treated as indications for intensified counseling, additional home visits, or supervised feeding rather than attributing fault to caregivers. This comprehensive approach ensures that adherence failures are managed effectively, reducing the risk of adverse outcomes in paediatric SAM patients.

case_studies.title: Managing Defaulting in a CMAM Program: A Case of Missed Visits and Clinical Deterioration
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India, enrolled in a CMAM program for severe acute malnutrition (SAM), has missed his last two scheduled visits. The child had been receiving ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) for three weeks but was reported to have poor weight gain during initial follow-ups. The ASHA worker conducts a home visit and finds the mother reporting that the child has been vomiting frequently after meals and refusing RUTF. The family admits to sharing some of the therapeutic food with other children due to financial constraints. On examination, the child appears lethargic and irritable, with visible wasting, sunken eyes, and mild bilateral pedal edema. The ASHA worker notes that the family has also been affected by recent flooding, which disrupted access to the CMAM center. No recent weight measurements are available, but the mother reports that the child has become less active over the past week. The ASHA worker escalates the case to the ANM for further review.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify and manage cases of defaulting and adherence failures in a CMAM program, including recognizing clinical danger signs and social barriers requiring urgent referral.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DISCHARGE DECISIONS WHEN MUAC AND WHZ DISAGREE
subtopics.concept: Discharge decisions for children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in outpatient Community-Based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) programs in India require careful consideration when Mid-Upper Arm Circumference (MUAC) and Weight-for-Height Z-score (WHZ/WLZ) measurements disagree. MUAC and WHZ identify overlapping but distinct high-risk groups, and discordant recovery between these indicators is common after prolonged treatment. Therefore, discharge decisions should be anchored to the admission criterion and repeated edema-free stability rather than relying on a single improved measurement. At enrollment, it is essential to document the criterion that triggered admission—MUAC <115 mm, WHZ/WLZ <-3, and/or nutritional edema—and use this same criterion as the primary recovery anchor. The World Health Organization (WHO) recovery standards recommend discharging children only when MUAC reaches ≥125 mm and there is no bilateral pitting edema for at least two consecutive visits, or when WHZ/WLZ improves to ≥-2 and edema is absent for at least two consecutive visits, depending on the program pathway. When MUAC and WHZ measurements disagree, discharge should not occur solely because one indicator normalizes. If WHZ improves to ≥-2 but MUAC remains <125 mm, therapeutic feeding and close follow-up must continue, as residual muscle wasting poses a significant mortality risk. Conversely, if MUAC normalizes but WHZ remains low, it is critical to reassess measurement quality and investigate underlying factors such as chronic infection, malabsorption, feeding difficulties related to disability, or household food sharing practices. Anthropometric recovery should be combined with functional recovery indicators, including sustained appetite, activity levels, and caregiver competence in safe feeding practices. In India, where community screening practices vary, documenting both MUAC and weight-for-height during clinic visits helps prevent premature discharge from one system while the child still meets SAM criteria in another. This integrated approach ensures comprehensive care and reduces the risk of adverse outcomes in children recovering from SAM.

subtopics.subtopic_title: OUTPATIENT RECOVERY CRITERIA AND MAXIMUM DURATION WITH NON-RESPONSE REFERRAL
subtopics.concept: In the outpatient management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) under the Community-Based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) framework in India, explicit recovery and exit criteria must be defined and labeled as "program cure," distinct from inpatient "transfer readiness." For outpatient recovery, the safest approach involves achieving anthropometric recovery alongside a sustained oedema-free period, as oedema can relapse and weight may fluctuate with intercurrent illnesses. A single primary recovery standard should be used consistently. The World Health Organization (WHO) recommends discharge criteria of mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) ≥125 mm with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least two weeks, or weight-for-height z-score (WHZ) ≥-2 with no bilateral pitting oedema for at least two weeks. Percentage weight gain (e.g., "15% gain") should not be used as a sole recovery criterion, as it is biased by admission weight, particularly in cases involving oedema, and does not ensure normalization of wasting risk. Both MUAC and weight should be documented at every visit for dosing and trend analysis, even if only one indicator is used for admission. To prevent silent treatment failure, programs must define a maximum duration of stay and non-response triggers. Indian state protocols typically allow a maximum outpatient treatment duration of 12–16 weeks, with referral for medical evaluation if recovery does not occur within this timeframe. Earlier referral is warranted if weight gain is less than 5 g/kg/day over several weeks despite verified adherence, if there is persistent or worsening oedema, repeated appetite-test failure, or recurrent infections. When a child is classified as a "non-responder," a structured evaluation should be initiated to identify potential underlying issues such as missed infections, chronic diarrhea or malabsorption, tuberculosis (TB), HIV, disability-related feeding difficulties, or unsafe home delivery of therapeutic feeds. These measures ensure timely identification of barriers to recovery and appropriate escalation of care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: INPATIENT DISCHARGE READINESS AND STEP-DOWN  HANDOVER CRITERIA
subtopics.category: DISCHARGE FOLLOW- UP AND PROGRAM QUALITY
subtopics.concept: In the management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM), inpatient discharge readiness marks the transition from a high-risk hospital environment to continued rehabilitation in a less monitored setting and should not be conflated with outpatient program cure criteria. Discharge readiness is defined by clinical stability and the ability to safely continue care outside the ward. According to Indian facility protocols, discharge readiness criteria include the resolution or clinical improvement of complications, the child being alert with a good appetite, and the ability to take prescribed feeds orally. Additionally, there should be no hypoglycemia or hypothermia for at least 24 hours, dehydration must be corrected without ongoing shock, and nutritional oedema should be clearly reducing or resolved. A practical marker for feeding readiness is sustained oral intake delivering approximately 120–130 kcal/kg/day with demonstrated weight gain of at least 5 g/kg/day over three consecutive days. Parenteral antibiotics should be completed before discharge unless a supervised step-down plan is in place. At discharge, the therapeutic feeding prescription, whether a local catch-up diet or ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) sachet dose, must be documented explicitly. Families should be provided with written instructions on “return today” danger signs, which include poor intake, repeated vomiting, fever, fast breathing, convulsions, lethargy, or new/worsening oedema. The first follow-up visit must be scheduled within seven days, and families should be linked to the nearest outpatient nutrition service. Referral pathways back to inpatient stabilization should also be documented for cases where appetite fails or complications recur. It is essential to counsel caregivers that early weight loss in oedematous SAM may reflect oedema resolution rather than clinical deterioration; decisions to escalate care should be guided by clinical status and oedema grade rather than weight alone. This structured approach ensures a safe and effective transition from inpatient care to community-based rehabilitation.

case_studies.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Assessing Discharge Readiness
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India was admitted to the pediatric ward with severe acute malnutrition, presenting with bilateral pitting oedema, lethargy, and a history of poor feeding for two weeks. Initial management included correction of dehydration, treatment of hypoglycemia, and initiation of therapeutic feeding with F-75 formula. After two weeks of inpatient care, his oedema has significantly reduced, and he is alert and active. Over the past three days, he has been consuming therapeutic feeds orally, achieving an intake of approximately 125 kcal/kg/day, and has demonstrated consistent weight gain of 6 g/kg/day. His temperature and blood glucose levels have remained stable for the last 48 hours. The course of antibiotics for his prior lower respiratory tract infection has been completed. The family expresses understanding of the nutrition plan and is eager to return home. The nearest outpatient nutrition service is 15 km away, and follow-up arrangements have been made. The caregiver is counseled on recognizing danger signs, but they express concern about managing feeding at home. The healthcare team must decide if the child is ready for discharge to outpatient care or requires further inpatient monitoring.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess readiness for inpatient discharge in a child with severe acute malnutrition based on clinical stability, nutritional progress, and caregiver preparedness.

subtopics.subtopic_title: DISCHARGE COUNSELING CHECKLIST AND OUTPATIENT PRESCRIPTION TEMPLATE
subtopics.concept: Discharge planning for children with Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) is a critical transition phase aimed at minimizing relapse, defaulting, and unsafe feeding practices at home. A structured discharge counseling checklist and outpatient prescription are essential components of this process. Before discharge or step-down, caregivers must demonstrate their ability to correctly prepare and administer the prescribed feed—whether a local catch-up diet or a Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food (RUTF) sachet schedule—ensuring proper frequency, safe storage, and hygiene. They should also show competence in administering prescribed medicines and micronutrients. Caregivers must be educated to recognize danger signs requiring same-day medical attention, such as poor intake, repeated vomiting, fever or hypothermia, fast breathing, convulsions, lethargy, or new or worsening edema, and they should be informed about the nearest referral facility. The outpatient prescription must outline a complete care plan rather than merely listing product names. It should specify the therapeutic food, daily dose (e.g., number of RUTF sachets per day and their division), continuation or completion dates for antibiotics if applicable, a micronutrient schedule with clear instructions on iron timing, and the exact date for the next follow-up visit, ideally within seven days. A tracing mechanism should be included, such as a directive to contact the family within 48–72 hours if the follow-up visit is missed, with the responsible cadre (e.g., ASHA, AWW, or ANM) explicitly named based on local program design. At discharge, document the child’s anthropometry and edema status and confirm whether high-dose vitamin A has been administered to avoid duplication. Families should also be counseled on managing situations where therapeutic food is temporarily unavailable and provided with clear escalation instructions if weight gain stalls or the child’s condition deteriorates. This structured approach ensures continuity of care and supports the child’s recovery in the outpatient setting.

case_studies.title: Severe Acute Malnutrition: Discharge Counseling and Outpatient Planning
case_studies.vignette: A 14-month-old boy from a rural village in India, weighing 5.6 kg (below -3 SD for weight-for-age), was admitted to the pediatric ward with severe acute malnutrition and bilateral pitting edema. Over the past two weeks, he has improved on therapeutic feeds and medical management. The edema has resolved, and he is now taking feeds orally. His mother, a daily wage laborer, expresses concern about maintaining his recovery at home. During discharge planning, the pediatrician observes that the mother struggles to measure the prescribed RUTF sachet portions and is unsure how to identify danger signs like fever or poor intake. The family lives 15 km from the nearest health center, and the father has irregular access to transportation. The mother is also unclear about the follow-up date and how to store the RUTF sachets safely in their one-room home without refrigeration. The team needs to finalize the discharge counseling and outpatient prescription to ensure compliance and avoid relapse.
case_studies.learning_objective: To understand the essential components of discharge counseling and outpatient planning for children with severe acute malnutrition, including caregiver education, prescription clarity, and follow-up mechanisms.

case_studies.title: Discharge Counseling for Severe Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 15-month-old boy from a rural village in India was admitted to the pediatric ward with severe acute malnutrition (weight-for-height Z-score < -3) and bilateral pedal edema. After 14 days of inpatient care, including therapeutic feeds and treatment for an associated respiratory infection, his edema resolved, and his appetite improved. He is now clinically stable, with no signs of fever, vomiting, or respiratory distress, and has transitioned to RUTF-based feeding. His weight has shown a steady gain of 6 grams/kg/day over the past week. The caregiver, his mother, appears attentive but expresses concern about correctly preparing the feeds and recognizing danger signs at home. The family lives 20 kilometers from the nearest health facility, with limited access to transportation. The discharge plan includes a follow-up visit at the outpatient therapeutic program (OTP) center in seven days. The mother is asked to demonstrate feed preparation and administration, but she hesitates, appearing unsure about the correct steps. Additionally, she is unaware of the signs that would require immediate medical attention.
case_studies.learning_objective: Understand the essential components of discharge counseling and outpatient prescription planning for children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) to minimize relapse and ensure safe home care.

subtopics.subtopic_title: MEASLES VACCINATION TIMING AND ROUTINE IMMUNIZATION CATCH-UP
subtopics.category: DISCHARGE FOLLOW- UP AND PROGRAM QUALITY
subtopics.concept: In India, measles vaccination is a critical component of the immunization strategy for children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM), given their heightened vulnerability to infections and the increased case fatality associated with measles in this group. Measles-containing vaccines are licensed for use in children aged 6 months and older. According to standard hospital guidance, children with SAM who are unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated should receive a measles-containing vaccine once they are clinically stable. However, vaccination should be delayed if the child is in shock or has uncontrolled acute instability. If a measles vaccine is administered before 9 months of age as an “early dose” for high-risk children, it is essential to ensure that the child receives the routine measles-containing doses as per the national immunization schedule after 9 months, as early vaccination may not confer durable immunity. The immunization schedule for children with SAM should be integrated into both inpatient and outpatient workflows. At the time of discharge from inpatient care, healthcare providers should document the child’s immunization card status, administer any due vaccines, and provide a clear plan for subsequent doses, including the date and location. In outpatient care, immunization status should be reassessed at each weekly visit, and families should be linked to routine immunization sessions. Missed immunization appointments should be treated as a follow-up failure risk, similar to missed therapeutic feeding visits. The efficacy and waning of immunity following measles vaccination in children with SAM were not specified in the source. Contraindications include the presence of shock or uncontrolled acute instability at the time of vaccination. For catch-up immunization, children who missed doses should receive them promptly once clinically stable, and vaccination timing should not be confused with vitamin A supplementation, which follows separate indication-based protocols. Special considerations for other groups were not specified in the source. Ensuring timely measles vaccination in children with SAM is a preventive action that can significantly reduce mortality and improve health outcomes.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CMAM PROGRAM PERFORMANCE INDICATORS AND SPHERE BENCHMARKS
subtopics.category: DISCHARGE FOLLOW- UP AND PROGRAM QUALITY
subtopics.concept: The performance indicators for Community-based Management of Acute Malnutrition (CMAM) programs and their alignment with Sphere benchmarks are critical for assessing program quality and ensuring patient safety in paediatric care. Poor cure rates and high defaulting often indicate systemic issues such as missed diagnoses, unreliable therapeutic food delivery, or delayed referrals. Performance benchmarks must be tailored to the specific setting, as inpatient stabilization units and outpatient CMAM programs exhibit different patterns of mortality and defaulting. For outpatient therapeutic feeding programs, Sphere standards recommend that recovery rates exceed 75%, mortality remains below 10%, and defaulting stays under 15% among discharges. Additional operational indicators, such as median length of stay, the proportion of children presenting with nutritional oedema at admission, and the fraction referred to inpatient care for complications, should also be tracked to predict individual treatment failure. In inpatient settings, mortality thresholds are expected to be lower than outpatient benchmarks when stabilization is timely and iatrogenic harm is minimized. However, local targets must account for the severity of referrals and the burden of comorbidities. High-yield quality indicators for inpatient care include deaths occurring within the first 48–72 hours, often linked to shock, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, or errors in fluid management, as well as inappropriate use of intravenous fluids and adherence to phased feeding and electrolyte protocols. A shared non-response dashboard across inpatient and outpatient settings is essential for monitoring treatment outcomes. Key indicators include failure to regain appetite by day 4 (inpatient), oedema not reducing by day 4 or persisting by day 10, and weight gain below 5 g/kg/day after initiating catch-up feeding. When these thresholds are exceeded, it signals a systemic issue requiring protocol audits, such as evaluating therapeutic food preparation, stock management, dosing accuracy, and referral pathways, rather than attributing blame to individual caregivers. These measures ensure comprehensive program monitoring and continuous quality improvement in paediatric SAM management.

subtopics.subtopic_title: FEEDING MANAGEMENT IN INFANTS UNDER 6 MONTHS
subtopics.category: INFANTS UNDER SIX MONTHS AND COMORBIDITY MODIFICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Feeding management in infants under six months with severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is a feeding-first, dyad-focused approach aimed at restoring effective breastfeeding or ensuring safe alternative feeding while preventing hypoglycemia, hypothermia, and infection. Direct breastfeeding should be prioritized, with skilled observation of attachment, positioning, and milk transfer. For weak infants unable to breastfeed effectively, expressed breast milk can be offered via cup or spoon while continuing attempts at direct breastfeeding. If breast milk supply is initially insufficient, relactation should be supported, and the supplementary suckling technique (SST) may be employed. SST involves the infant suckling at the breast while receiving supplemental milk through a thin tube, which simultaneously increases intake and stimulates milk production. Starter therapeutic feeds should only be used temporarily when breast milk is inadequate, avoiding overly concentrated formulas that increase solute load. For non-breastfed infants with no prospects of breastfeeding, program-approved replacement feeding should be used, accompanied by strict hygiene counseling and close follow-up. If safe preparation of replacement feeds cannot be ensured, this should be treated as an indication for hospital admission. Acute problems should be stabilized using standard SAM safety rules, including frequent small feeds to avoid fasting, maintaining warmth, early treatment of suspected infections, and avoiding unnecessary intravenous fluids. Daily reassessment must evaluate feeding effectiveness, urine output as a proxy for hydration, temperature, weight trends, and maternal barriers to care. Discharge criteria include effective feeding on observation, clinical stability, and clear weight gain on the home-feeding plan. Weekly follow-up should be arranged until sustained weight gain is demonstrated. Caregivers must be counseled on return-to-care danger signs, including poor feeding, lethargy, fever or hypothermia, fast breathing, vomiting, or new edema.

case_studies.title: Feeding Management in a 3-Month-Old with Severe Acute Malnutrition
case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to a rural health center in India by his 19-year-old mother, who reports that the baby has been crying excessively and appears increasingly lethargic over the past week. The mother states that she has struggled with breastfeeding since birth and feels her milk supply is insufficient. The infant has had no fever, vomiting, or diarrhea but has passed scant urine and is feeding poorly. On examination, the infant weighs 3.2 kg (birth weight: 2.8 kg), has visible wasting, a sunken anterior fontanelle, and cold extremities. He is irritable but lethargic, with a weak suck and poor responsiveness during breastfeeding attempts observed in the clinic. The mother appears anxious and reports feeling overwhelmed but is willing to continue breastfeeding if supported. The health center lacks advanced neonatal care, but basic feeding support and observation are available. Capillary blood glucose is 52 mg/dL, and axillary temperature is 35.8°C.
case_studies.learning_objective: Assess and initiate appropriate feeding management in an infant under 6 months with severe acute malnutrition, focusing on breastfeeding support, prevention of complications, and criteria for hospital admission.

subtopics.subtopic_title: SAM MANAGEMENT MODIFICATIONS IN MAJOR COMORBIDITIES
subtopics.category: INFANTS UNDER SIX MONTHS AND COMORBIDITY MODIFICATIONS
subtopics.concept: Management of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in infants under six months requires specific modifications when major comorbidities are present, as these conditions alter risks and necessitate adjustments to the standard treatment protocol. In cases of suspected or confirmed tuberculosis (TB), a low threshold for screening should be maintained in non-responders, as a negative tuberculin skin test (TST) cannot reliably rule out TB. Coordination with the National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP) is essential for diagnostics and follow-up, and pyridoxine prophylaxis should be provided alongside isoniazid-containing regimens. Weight recovery in these infants may be slower than usual. For HIV-exposed or infected infants, staged feeding and routine potassium/magnesium supplementation should continue, while empiric antibiotics remain necessary even if cotrimoxazole prophylaxis is already in place. If severe pneumonia with profound hypoxia suggests Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP), high-dose cotrimoxazole should be added per protocol. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) should be initiated only after stabilization of sepsis and metabolic complications, with linkage to the nearest ART center for continuity of care. Infants with neurodisability or unsafe swallowing require careful management of aspiration risk, including slower feeding, head elevation, early nasogastric (NG) support with strict placement confirmation, and extended inpatient observation. Caregiver competency in safe feeding techniques is a mandatory prerequisite for discharge. For congenital heart disease or chronic lung disease, feeding advancement should be slower, additional fluids restricted, and respiratory rate and pulse closely monitored during feed escalation. Early cardiology input is advisable. In cases of chronic kidney disease (CKD) or chronic liver disease (CLD), nephrotoxic antibiotics should be avoided when alternatives exist, electrolytes interpreted cautiously, and phosphate replacement reduced or closely monitored if refeeding syndrome is suspected. Referral thresholds should be lowered when close monitoring cannot be ensured. These modifications underscore the importance of tailoring SAM management to the dominant comorbidity, emphasizing individualized care to optimize outcomes in this vulnerable population.
